content.problemStatement:
You are a generous parent and have a collection of cookies to give to your children. Each child can receive **at most one cookie**.

Each child iii has a **greed factor** g[i]g[i]g[i], which is the minimum size of a cookie that will make the child happy. Each cookie jjj has a **size** s[j]s[j]s[j]. A child will be content if the size of the cookie they receive is **greater than or equal to** their greed factor.

Your task is to **maximize the number of content children** and return that number.

Function DeclarationFunction NamemaxContentChildrenmaxContentChildrenmaxContentChildren – This function calculates the maximum number of children that can be made content by distributing cookies such that each child receives at most one cookie, and the cookie size is at least the child’s greed factor.

Parameters
ggg : A reference to an integer array representing the greed factor of each child.
sss : A reference to an integer array representing the sizes of available cookies.
Return Value
Returns an integer representing the maximum number of content children.
Constraints
1≤T≤101 \leq T \leq 101≤T≤10
1≤n≤3×1041 \leq n \leq 3 \times 10^41≤n≤3×104 (number of children)
0≤m≤3×1040 \leq m \leq 3 \times 10^40≤m≤3×104 (number of cookies)
1≤g[i],s[j]≤231−11 \leq g[i], s[j] \leq 2^{31} - 11≤g[i],s[j]≤231−1

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains two integers nnn and mmm — number of children and number of cookies.
The next line contains nnn integers — greed factors of the children.
The next line contains mmm integers — sizes of the cookies.




Output Format

For each test case, output a single integer — the maximum number of content children.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two integers n and m, the number of children and the number of cookies, followed by two lines containing the greed factors of the children and the sizes of the cookies.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the maximum number of content children.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: After sorting children [1,2,3,4] and cookies [1,2,3], the first three children can each receive a cookie, so 3 are content. Test case 2: Children [1,2,2] can all be satisfied with cookies [1,2,3,4,5], giving 3 content children.
Both children have greed 5 and 6 and there are cookies of exactly those sizes, so both children become content.
Each child can receive a cookie of the same size as their greed factor, so all 3 children become content.

solution.approach: The key insight is to sort both the greed factors and the cookie sizes. Then, we use two pointers to iterate through the sorted arrays. If the current cookie size is greater than or equal to the current greed factor, we increment the count of content children and move to the next child. We always move to the next cookie, regardless of whether the current child is content or not. This approach ensures that we maximize the number of content children.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (n log n + m log m)) per test case, O(T * (n log n + m log m)) total. The sorting operations dominate the time complexity.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) - excluding the space required for the input and output, the space complexity is constant.

code.Python3:
import sys

def max_content_children(g, s):
    g.sort()
    s.sort()
    i = j = 0
    while i < len(g) and j < len(s):
        if s[j] >= g[i]:
            i += 1
        j += 1
    return i

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().splitlines()
    if not data:
        return
    t = int(data[0])
    idx = 1
    out = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n, m = map(int, data[idx].split()); idx += 1
        g = list(map(int, data[idx].split())) if n else []
        idx += 1
        s = list(map(int, data[idx].split())) if m else []
        idx += 1
        out.append(str(max_content_children(g, s)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
